text: Over the past decade, China has witnessed a series of civic movements targeting the production of para-xylene (PX) successfully and peacefully in Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo. The violent anti-PX campaign in Maoming in 2014 undoubtedly broke the pattern. By combining ethnographic methods of participant observation and in-depth interviews, this paper looks into the special dynamics behind the Maoming campaign. Our fieldwork shows that despite the governmental campaigns promoting the PET industry, people in Maoming have already acquired, through their long-term experiences with local chemical plants and their extensive listening to and engaging with civic movements related to PX in other cities, the rudiments of environmental risk awareness. Different from the movements organized and participated by the urban middle class, Maoming did not have effective leaders or groups to control the violent activities, which made the Maoming anti-PX campaign especially unreasonable and hard to be contained by the state. Meanwhile, by drawing on examples from the use of social media in urban space during the campaigns, we argue that despite the governmental imposition of a ban on reports on the social media on the campaigns and the lack of coverage and participation of non-governmental organizations in the civic organizations can also use smart mobile devices to organize crowds to overthrow authorities in their own familiar home city. Finally, our case study also provides a critical look on the obstacles and ways of organizing environment-related social movements in contemporary China.
explanation: The text uses formal, research-oriented phrasing typical of academic abstracts, but contains some awkward and slightly off constructions that can indicate AI-generated text rather than carefully human-authored prose.
